coredata.dc:title: Experimental study on liquid immersion preheating of lithium-ion batteries under low temperature environment
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Bao, Jiakang
Zhou, Zhifu
Wu, Wei-Tao
Wei, Lei
Lyu, Jizu
Li, Yang
Huang, Heng
Li, Yubai
Song, Yongchen
coredata.dc:description: An experimental platform to examine the effects of single-phase immersion preheating on lithium-ion battery performance at low temperatures was set up in this study. The performance of lithium-ion batteries at low temperatures can be improved through immersion preheating. After preheating from −15 °C to 15 °C, the battery capacity can recover to over 80 % of its rated capacity. The three influencing factors are inlet flow rate, fluid temperature, and cell spacings. The inlet temperature exerts the most influence on the preheating performance. The warming rate can reach 1.63 °C/min at a temperature of 50 °C. While the increase in flow rate has less influence on the heating rate, it has a greater impact on the temperature difference between cells. The maximum temperature difference between cells can still be kept below 4 °C. The impact of spacing on the heating rate and temperature difference is minimal. The temperature difference can be controlled within 6 °C. Finally, it's important to note that immersion preheating consumes significant amount of energy. The energy consumption of preheating at an ambient temperature of −25 °C exceeds 80 % of the rated capacity of the experimental battery pack.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Immersion preheating
Energy consumption
Cold start
Low temperature

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Battery performance in the low-temperature environment
Changes in battery performance after low-temperature preheating
Analysis of battery immersion preheating at different ambient temperatures
The preheating performance of immersion preheating at different ambient temperatures
Analysis of energy consumption for immersion preheating
The extent to which different elements affect the immersion preheating of batteries

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Battery immersion preheating test system: (a) test flow and principle, (b) battery pack module, (c) thermocouple pasting position.
Battery charging and discharging voltage curves and capacity changes at different temperatures: (a) discharge voltage, (b) discharge capacity, (c) charge voltage, (d) charge capacity.
Battery discharge voltage curve and capacity change after preheating: (a) discharge voltage, (b) discharge capacity (compared with 25 °C discharge capacity).
Change in minimum and maximum temperature difference after single-cell warm-up: (a) temperature change, (b) temperature difference change.

Battery pack temperature, the maximal difference in temperature change during warm-up to 15 °C: (a) average temperature and minimum temperature, (b) differential cell temperatures, differential pack temperatures pack.

Changes in minimum and maximum differences in temperature for packs on batteries preheating under different ambient temperatures: (a) minimum temperature, (b) cell temperature difference, (c) battery pack temperature difference.
Energy consumption of battery pack preheating at different temperatures.
Affect in inlet flow rate on battery immersion preheating performance.
Influence of inlet fluid temperature on cell immersion preheating performance.
Effect of cell separation distance on cell immersion preheating performance.

row.entry:
Fast heat-up, low energy consumption
Complexity of production

Fast warming and high safety
Complex control systems

Low cost and high security
Damage to the battery

Fast heating with low-temperature difference
High sealing requirements

head.ce:title: Experimental study on liquid immersion preheating of lithium-ion batteries under low temperature environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An experimental platform to examine the effects of single-phase immersion preheating on lithium-ion battery performance at low temperatures was set up in this study. The performance of lithium-ion batteries at low temperatures can be improved through immersion preheating. After preheating from −15 °C to 15 °C, the battery capacity can recover to over 80 % of its rated capacity. The three influencing factors are inlet flow rate, fluid temperature, and cell spacings. The inlet temperature exerts the most influence on the preheating performance. The warming rate can reach 1.63 °C/min at a temperature of 50 °C. While the increase in flow rate has less influence on the heating rate, it has a greater impact on the temperature difference between cells. The maximum temperature difference between cells can still be kept below 4 °C. The impact of spacing on the heating rate and temperature difference is minimal. The temperature difference can be controlled within 6 °C. Finally, it's important to note that immersion preheating consumes significant amount of energy. The energy consumption of preheating at an ambient temperature of −25 °C exceeds 80 % of the rated capacity of the experimental battery pack.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:def-description.ce:para: maximum temperature difference of battery pack (°C)
maximal temperature in the battery pack (°C)
minimal temperature in the battery pack (°C)

ce:section.ce:para: Since the beginning of the new century, energy structure and environmental issues have prompted countries around the world to focus on new energy development. Energy storage technology is the core of new energy development. It will become the leading technology driving the global energy revolution [1–3]. Among them, the lithium-ion battery (LIB) is being widely employed in the electrochemical energy storage sector as an energy storage device with excellent performance, including high power density, high energy density, and low self-discharge rates [4,5]. The performance of LIB is highly dependent on the temperature. The best working temperature for LIB ranges from 25 to 40 °C. And the temperature non-uniformity for LIB should be no more than 5 °C [6,7]. Under high-temperature conditions, the heating from the discharge of a LIB can seriously affect cycling life. It can even cause a violent exothermic reaction within the battery, leading to thermal runaway or even explosion [8–10]. At low temperatures, the electrochemical reaction rate of the LIB slows down and the viscosity of the electrolyte increases, resulting in a decrease in the performance of the LIB [11–13]. During charging at low temperatures, Li+ can precipitate as lithium metal on the anode electrode electrolyte interface. These deposits may evolve into dendritic crystals, which can pierce through the battery separators, potentially causing internal short circuits or even explosions [14]. Furthermore, temperature consistency is an essential factor in LIB capacity [15,16]. Feng et al. [17] concluded from modeling analysis that the battery pack loses 1.5 %–2 % capacity for every 5 °C temperature difference. Therefore, building an efficient battery thermal management system (BTMS) is essential for cold-starting a LIB.

Wang et al. [18] summarized different preheating methods and techniques, categorizing the low-temperature preheating of LIB into internal and external preheating based on their heat transfer mechanisms. They also discussed the advantages and disadvantages of these methods. Internal heating refers to the electric reaction heat of the battery itself or the use of electric heating elements to generate heat out of the inner part of cells to achieve the purpose of preheating [19]. There are three main heating methods for internal preheating: battery-embedded element heating, pulse preheating, and alternating current preheating. Wang et al. [20,21] improved the battery construction by embedding a nickel foil inside the battery and connecting a circuit as an internal electrical heating element to preheat the battery in cold conditions. This is a self-heating battery. This preheating with embedded heating can heat the battery from around −30 °C–10 °C in less than 2 min and requires energy from less than 2 % of the capacity of the battery. And the battery loses only 7.2 % of its capacity at −30 °C after 500 rapid preheat cycles. However, the changes from the inside construction bring difficulties to the quality testing in series and line production of batteries. Unlike self-heating, reciprocal pulse heating and alternating current (AC) heating utilize the heat of the LIB's electrochemical reaction itself for preheating. Xiong et al. [22] utilized it for heating the battery by using high-force alternation on the secondary aspect of the linear electrical power supply system. This preheating method can take the battery from −20 °C to 0 °C in 14 s. The average heating rate is about 8.5 °C/min. Zhang et al. [23] compared the preheating effects of different parameters during AC preheating and found that a lower frequency and higher current amplitude could enhance the heating rate. There is no obvious capacity loss of the battery after several preheating periods, which indicates that AC preheating with proper parameter selection will not cause lithium plating.

External preheating can be categorized into four types: phase change material (PCM) preheating, liquid preheating, air preheating, and electric heating element preheating. Among them, liquid preheating and electric heating element preheating are currently the mainstream methods. Electric heater elements are characterized by a high heating capacity and a complex system of simple construction. Li et al. [24] used a heating method for heating batteries with aluminum plates wound with positive temperature coefficient (PTC) resistive wires. The battery module was capable of being heated over 25 min to 0 °C from −40 °C when the PTC heater was delivering 35 W of heating power. Lei et al. [25] improved the electric voltage and output capabilities of a battery cell under a 1C (C rate is the measurement of the charge and discharge current for its nominal capacity) charge/discharge multiplier after heating the battery for 15 min at 90 W–240 W power using a wide wire metal film. Preheating of electric heating elements does not have a clear advantage over other preheating methods in terms of temperature difference, which can result in excessive temperature gradients [26]. Liquids have excellent thermal conductivity and specific heat capacity [27], which have a broader prospect in practical applications [28]. For example, Volt and Tesla use liquid indirect preheating for battery thermal management [29,30]. Zhu et al. [31] designed a thermal management system, that utilized a flat plate-type heat exchanger for heat transfer from a battery pack. This system can achieve warming at a rate of 0.55 °C/min and a maximal temperature difference of 5.7 °C while heating a cell at low temperatures. Qian et al. [32] investigated how the flow channels are distributed from the inlet into the frozen panel. The results of the study show that the higher the number of flow channels, the more uniformity of fluid distribution. It also reduces the cell temperature and temperature difference. Kausthubharam et al. [33] presented a diagonal-type minichannel-based thermal management system. This method allows the maximum temperature of the battery pack to be 20 % lower than the temperature at the upper limit of the operating range.

Liquid immersion thermal management, on the other hand, can achieve higher heat transfer coefficients and more uniform temperature distribution and has shown great potential in thermal management [34]. Wang et al. [35] constructed an experimental platform to investigate the cooling performance by using insulating material No. 10 transformer oil as an immersion coolant. Under the conditions of inlet flux with 0.8 L/min and 2 C discharge, the top temperature is controlled at 39.4 °C. Moreover, the maximum temperature difference was reduced from 4.97 °C to 1.23 °C. Wang et al. [36] introduced a new way of immersion chilling. With the cell submerged in the static stream, cooling tubes in close contact with the fluid are set up inside. The cooling tubes transfer the emitted heat from the cell using the cooling liquid. This approach resulted in a 150.3 % and 45.7 % enhancement of the best operating temperature limits compared to natural convection and immersion chilling. Phase change immersion offers much better heat transfer properties and more uniform temperature consistency than single-phase submergence. Hirano et al. [37] immersed an experimental cell in a liquid of HF acid ether for thermal management studies. The results showed that the temperature could be controlled at 35 °C even when the cell is subjected to conditions of charging and discharging at high rate. Li et al. [38–41] investigated the boiling phenomenon as well as the cooling effect during submergence of SF33 in Li-ion batteries. As a conclusion, the cell temperature was controlled to be at 34.5 °C or less, despite being discharged at a rate of 7C. In addition, immersion cooling has a significant inhibitory effect on the thermal runaway of LIB [42]. In this case, the dielectric fluid absorbs a large amount of heat and prevents or retards the temperature rise of the battery. At the same time, it prevents heat conduction to the surrounding battery and the spread of thermal runaway [34].

However, there are few studies on liquid immersion preheating [43]. Luo et al. [44] designed an immersion heating system using electric heating oil, where an electric heating film is placed at the bottom of the battery pack to indirectly preheat the battery by heating the liquid. Test data show that the rate of warming is up to 0.85 °C/min. When the battery discharge multiplier is small, continuous heating is required to maintain the battery temperature. Moreover, the temperature difference between cells after warming up is limited to 5 °C, and the temperature difference is limited to 3 °C by liquid circulation or static storage. Wang et al. [45] investigated the impact of inlet velocity and charging power on the heating characteristics of battery packs in an immersion preheating system. The experimental data indicates inlet velocity has almost no impact on heating time and has a large impact on heat uniformity for batteries and single cells. When heating power was equal, the battery pack's utmost thermal differences were controlled at less than 3 °C under the conditions of 500 W heating power and a 1.25 m/s flow rate. Table 1 summarizes the different preheating methods. The heating rate and temperature uniformity of different preheating are also compared.
2.% means that the total energy consumed by the preheating process accounts for in the total battery capacity.

Immersion thermal management has shown great potential in battery cooling, but there has been limited research on immersion preheating as a heating technique. Therefore, this paper presents an experimental analysis of the enhancement in low-temperature battery performance achieved through immersion preheating and the impact of variables such as inlet medium temperature, fluid flow rate, and cell spacing on the preheating process. Additionally, the efficacy of submerged preheating is thoroughly investigated by both quantitatively and qualitatively assessing the experimental outcomes. This study aims to understand the key performance indicators of preheating and to analyze the factors that contribute to performance, which is crucial for the application and development of immersion thermal management.

In this section, the experimental system is described, including the test cell module, the heating fluid, the instrumentation, and the preheating cycle. The operational procedures and uncertainty analysis of the experiment are described in detail.

In this experiment, a lithium iron phosphate battery was used for the test. The LIB uses lithium iron phosphate as the positive material and carbon as the negative material. The LIB possesses a nominal capacity of 32 Ah and a nominal voltage of 3.2 V, with a charging cut-off voltage of 3.65 V. Specific parameters for the LIB are shown in Table 2 .

Dimethyl silicone oil was chosen as the immersion medium for the experiment. Silicone oil is a polymer composed of silicon and oxygen atoms with insulation, high-temperature stability, cold resistance, and other characteristics. It is specially used for mechanical lubrication, thermal conductivity, insulation, and experimental oil baths. Battery thermal safety can be greatly improved by the immersion cooling strategy. Specific parameters of the silicone oil are shown in Table 3 . Silicone oil density variation with temperature measurement results is shown in Table 4 [45].

The focus of this experiment is to study the improvement of preheating on the LIB under low-temperature conditions and how different factors affect preheating. During the LIB preheating experiment, the LIB is enclosed within a box, with each cell fully immersed in silicone oil. The silicone oil possesses superior thermal conductivity and thermal stability, significantly mitigating the safety risks associated with the battery pack. Before the start of the experiment, the temperature chamber was adjusted to the desired test temperature until the cell temperature monitored by the thermocouple stabilized (fluctuation less than 0.5 °C). Concurrently, the silicone oil underwent preheating to reach the designated temperature. Subsequently, the oil pump will draw hot silicone oil and circulate it into the LIB compartment. The rapid fluid flow enhances heat transfer in the LIB, causing a rapid increase in battery temperature. The heated silicone oil is then returned to the thermostatic bath for reheating, and the process continues until the entire battery pack reaches the target preheating temperature. The experimental apparatus other than the computer was turned off, while thermocouples recorded temperature data on a computer to record the temperature data for subsequent analysis. During the discharge performance test of the LIB post-preheating, once the LIB achieves the desired preheating temperature, deactivate the oil pump and thermostat tank to end the cycle. Subsequently, the battery test equipment is engaged to initiate discharge and ensure that the battery data is logged into the computer for future analysis.

The inlet flow rate is 0.5–2.5 L/min, the inlet fluid temperature is 30–50 °C, the outlet boundary condition is set as pressure outlet, and the outlet boundary condition is set as pressure outlet. The thermal deformation of the cell is not considered in the heat transfer process. The boundary conditions on the outer wall surface of the battery box are set to be thermally insulated due to the insulation used in the battery box. The immersion preheating experiment designed in this work is carried out in a relatively closed thermostat, and the external connecting pipeline is insulated, so it is less affected by the outside world and has high repeatability. The control equations and important variables involved in the experimental procedure are shown below. The fluid mass conservation equation is shown as Eq. (1). The fluid momentum equation is shown as Eq. (2). The fluid energy equation is shown as Eq. (3). And Eq. (4) is the energy conservation equation for the power cell [45].

The important performance parameters monitored during the experiment are the rate of temperature rise (Eq. (5)), the maximal temperature difference in the cell (Eq. (6)), and the maximal temperature difference in the battery pack (Eq. (7)). Parameters can be calculated using the following:

To ensure the credibility of the experimental data, the uncertainty of the collected data needs to be analyzed. The uncertainties of the experimental data can be calculated by Eq. (8), where ∂ψ

2
The performance of immersion preheating can be affected by many factors. An experimental platform was used to study preheating and investigate the effect of different factors on preheating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Battery performance in the low-temperature environment
ce:section.ce:para: Different temperatures (−25 °C, −15 °C, −5 °C, 5 °C, 15 °C, 25 °C) were established using a temperature chamber to conduct charge/discharge tests on the LIB. The purpose was to show how these temperature variations influenced the voltage and capacity characteristics of the LIB, as indicated by the experimental findings. The charging process is carried out in CC-CV mode. During the constant current (CC) phase, the current is maintained at 32 A, and the cutoff voltage is set to 3.65 V. In the subsequent phase of the constant voltage (CV), the cutoff current is decreased to 1.6 A. Discharging is done using the CC mode with a current of 32 A and a cutoff voltage of 2.40 V. This result can be seen in Fig. 2 .

Fig. 2 (a) illustrates the variation of the discharge voltages of LIB at different temperatures. The battery voltage drops very slowly when the temperature is above 0 °C and reaches about 2.9 V when the voltage drops sharply to the cutoff voltage of 2.4 V. The decline in voltage before the complete discharge of LIB energy is due to a low state of charge (SOC) value and high internal resistance, which results in a sharp decrease in LIB discharge voltage. After the temperature is lower than 0 °C, the discharge voltage curve exhibits a pattern of initial decrease, followed by an increase, and then another decrease. This phenomenon is mainly due to the cold discharge at the beginning. As the internal resistance increases, LIB migration resistance increases, resulting in a sudden voltage drop. With the discharge process, LIB temperature increases, and the rate of LIB chemical reaction increases. The sequence of events leads to a gradual increase in the discharge voltage of the LIB [47]. The charging voltage plot can be seen in Fig. 2 (c). It can be observed that the rate of recharge increases as the temperature decreases. When it is under 0 °C, the battery voltage at the beginning of charging rapidly rises to the cut-off voltage, with no constant current charging process occurring. It then directly enters the continuous voltage charging phase. The duration required for constant current charging diminishes rapidly, while the rate of constant voltage charging escalates swiftly. Consequently, the time needed to charge an equivalent capacity also increases.

The experimental test indicated a 33.21 Ah discharge capacity at 25 °C, which is 104 % of the rated capacity. Therefore, the capacity variation observed in the experiment is based on the battery's capacity at 25 °C. From Fig. 2 (b) and Fig. 2 (d), it is evident that the cell capacity is greatly affected by temperature, particularly below 0 °C where battery capacity decreases rapidly. At −25 °C, the discharge capacity is only 23.8 % of 25 °C. This phenomenon is due to the battery electrolyte under cold conditions. It results in increased viscosity, reduced conductivity, increased resistance to lithium ion diffusion in LIB, and slower rates of electrochemical reactions. Consequently, the anode's utilization rate in the LIB is directly impacted, resulting in a diminution of the battery's overall capacity [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Changes in battery performance after low-temperature preheating
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, the position 3 battery in Fig. 1 (a) was selected as the research object. Set the temperature of silicone oil to 40 °C and the entrance volume of flow was 2.0 L/min. The distance between the cells was fixed at 2 mm. To analyze the voltage characteristics, a 1C discharge test was performed after heating the cells to −15 °C. This process was repeated at different low temperatures: −5 °C, 5 °C, 15 °C, and 25 °C. Experimental data on the test set appear in Fig. 3 . After the LIB was preheated by immersion in silicone oil, its voltage characteristics were significantly improved compared with the voltage at −15 °C. The initial voltage recovered to 2.95 V, 2.98 V, 3.03 V, and 3.10 V, indicating a substantial increase, and the capacity of the LIB was recovered. During the experiment, the LIB temperature reaches a target temperature and stops heating. Despite the insulation treatment applied to the outer wall of the box, after the LIB and the submerged medium are still with the outside world for heat transfer, the temperature is still slowly decreasing. Therefore, the voltage at discharge when targeting a higher preheating temperature is lower than the voltage at the actual target temperature. When the preheat target temperature is low, the silicone oil is still warmer than the cell and maintains a large temperature difference from the cell, even though the silicone oil is lower. This causes the temperature of the LIB to keep rising for a long time after the heating is stopped, and the discharging voltage can be even higher than that of a normal discharging process of a LIB at the same temperatures, as can be seen in Figs. 3 (a) −5 °C voltage curve. The initial voltage and discharge time are 2.95 V, 2.98 V, 2220 s, and 2280 s for preheating to −5 °C compared with 5 °C, which are almost the same. The increase in battery capacity is similar to that of voltage, as in Fig. 3 (b). The capacity for heating to 5 °C, 15 °C, and 25 °C only reaches 63.3 %, 81.6 %, and 88.3 % of that at 25 °C. It is smaller than the battery discharge capacity at the same temperature, with the disparity exceeding 10 % in all instances. However, the capacity reaches 59.9 % when heated to −5 °C, and the discharge capacity is higher than that of the LIB at the same temperature. When preheated to 15 °C, the capacity of the LIB was restored to 80 % of that at 25 °C. Therefore, in subsequent experiments investigating the effects of various factors, the preheating target temperature is set at 15 °C. It can be seen that the immersion preheating has a significant improvement on the low-temperature performance of the battery.

Fig. 4 shows the temperature variation of the cell during the preheat discharging process. The time required for cell heating increases as the target temperature rises. Notably, reaching a target temperature of 25 °C demands approximately 4000 s, significantly exceeding the duration needed for other temperatures. When the target temperature is lower than 15 °C, the temperature difference between the silicone oil and the cell is always large, so the cell warming rate is large. Specifically, the heating rates are 1.63 °C/min, 1.92 °C/min, and 1.34 °C/min for the target temperatures of −5 °C, 5 °C, and 15 °C, respectively. However, when the target temperature was 25 °C, the temperature difference from the fluid to the cell gradually decreased, causing a decline in the warming rate to 0.64 °C/min. Fig. 4 (b) shows the variation of the cell temperature difference during the preheating process, with the maximum temperature difference appearing at close times. In the case of lower target temperatures, such as the −5 °C temperature profile, the maximum temperature difference is significantly lower than in other cases because the heating time is only 345 s and the temperature difference has stopped heating before reaching the maximum value. Although the temperature difference reaches more than 10 °C at the initial moment, the temperature difference at the end of the preheating is controlled to be around 6 °C, which shows that immersion preheating has a great advantage in terms of cell temperature consistency.

Fig. 5 shows the temperature change of the battery pack at the target temperature of 15 °C. According to Fig. 5 (a), it can be seen that the minimum temperature curve is densely distributed with the average temperature curve, which indicates that the LIB temperature consistency is better. At the same time, the trend of the minimum temperature curve is similar, indicating that the battery warming up rates are comparable. The experimental data show that the battery warming rates are 1.02 °C/min, 1.12 °C/min, and 0.91 °C/min, respectively. Fig. 5 (b) shows that the closer to the inlet cell the better the homogeneity is for the temperature difference variations of the cell and the battery pack. Specifically, the temperature difference at the position 1 battery is maintained below 2 °C, while the outermost battery exhibits a temperature difference of up to 5 °C. While the temperature difference of the battery pack reaches 6 °C, which is caused by the uneven flow of silicone oil at different locations, the temperature difference between the batteries quickly drops to less than 2 °C at the end of the warm-up. The literature review of different preheating methods is summarized in Table 1. The advantages of immersion preheating compared to other preheating methods are reflected in the consistency of cell temperature and thermal safety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of battery immersion preheating at different ambient temperatures
The preheating performance of immersion preheating at different ambient temperatures
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the preheating performance of immersion preheating at different temperatures, the experiment was set up with a silicone oil temperature of 40 °C, a flow rate of 2.0 L/min, a cell spacing of 2 mm, and a preheating target temperature of 15 °C. A temperature chamber was used to simulate different ambient temperatures (−25 °C, −15 °C, −5 °C, and 5 °C), and the preheating effect of the battery pack was measured separately. The battery preheating temperature data for different initial ambient temperatures is shown in Fig. 6 . It is evident in Fig. 6 (a) that when preheating is performed up to different ambient temperatures, the temperature changes show similar curves, with the slopes gradually diminishing following the cessation of heating. Although a greater heating time is required at decreasing atmospheric temperatures, the low ambient temperature has a large temperature difference and warming rate at the preliminary stage of heating. This renders the average warming rates nearly identical across different temperatures (0.91 °C/min, 0.94 °C/min, 1.02 °C/min, 0.91 °C/min). The temperature difference is shown in Fig. 6 (b) and Fig. 6 (c). The lower the ambient temperature, the greater the temperature difference of the battery. At an ambient temperature of 25 °C, the maximum temperature difference between the battery and the battery pack was approximately 16 °C. However, by the end of the experiment, this difference could be reduced to below 8 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of energy consumption for immersion preheating
ce:section.ce:para: Although the performance of the battery will be enhanced and improved after heating at low temperatures, the level of energy consumption of the heating process is also an important reference standard for preheating. The energy consumed by submerged preheating is mainly divided into three parts: one is the fluid flow consumption, the second is the consumption of the heating fluid, and the third part is that of the cell for warming up. The energy consumption is calculated based on the power consumption of the pump and the temperature variation of the silicone oil and the cell. The calculated energy consumption of the preheating target temperature of 15 °C for various ambient temperatures is shown in Fig. 7 . The lower the ambient temperature, the higher the preheating energy consumption. The energy required for heating at temperatures of 5 °C, −5 °C, −15 °C, and −25 °C is 0.19 kWh, 0.27 kWh, 0.35 kWh, and 0.44 kWh, respectively. At −25 °C, this energy consumption exceeds 80 % of the rated battery capacity. Compared to other preheating methods, the energy consumption is higher. For instance, the total energy required to heat a battery with a metal film constitutes approximately 13 % of the battery's capacity [26]. In contrast, the energy consumption for LIB preheated through constant current discharge amounts to 30 %, while it is 15 % at 2 C and 1 C magnification discharges respectively [49]. Self-heating battery warm-up energy consumption from −30 °C to 10 °C is less than 2 % of battery capacity [20]. The main reason for this is that immersion preheating has a low utilization of heat, and a large part of the heat in the heating process is lost in heat exchange with the outside world.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The extent to which different elements affect the immersion preheating of batteries

ce:section.ce:para: The experiments were set up with a silicone oil temperature of 40 °C, a cell spacing of 2 mm, a preheating target temperature of 15 °C, a constant temperature and humidity chamber setting of −5 °C, and different values of inlet flow rate (0.5 L/min, 1.0 L/min, 1.5 L/min, 2.0 L/min, and 2.5 L/min). Experimental results are shown in Fig. 8 .

As shown in Fig. 8, it can be seen that in the case of small inlet flow rates of 0.5 L/min, 1.0 L/min, and 1.5 L/min, there is a substantial enhancement in the warming rates as the flow rate increases. The warming rate ascends from 0.64 °C/min to 0.91 °C/min. As the flow rate increases, the incremental effect on the rate of warming diminishes. Specifically, when the flow rate escalates from 1.5 L/min to 2.5 L/min, the rate of warming merely augments by 0.10 °C/min. For a single cell, the increase in flow rate does not change the temperature difference significantly, and the temperature difference is controlled to be under 4 °C. For the maximum temperature difference of the battery pack, increasing the flow rate will have a larger effect on the temperature difference. When the flow rate is increased from 0.5 L/min to 1.0 L/min, the temperature difference decreases from 4.1 °C to 3.8 °C, indicating that a higher flow rate contributes to a reduction in temperature discrepancy. When the flow rate is higher, such as the data from 1.5 L/min to 2.5 L/min in the figure, the temperature difference increases from 4.3 °C to 6.9 °C, and the flow rate increases the temperature inhomogeneity instead. The main reason for this phenomenon is that increasing the flow rate amplifies the disparity in fluid heat transfer across different cell gaps, thereby significantly affecting the temperature gradient within the battery pack.

The heat transfer fluid in the experiment was set to various temperatures (30 °C, 35 °C, 40 °C, 45 °C, and 50 °C), with an inlet flow rate of 2.0 L/min, a cell spacing of 2 mm, and an initial cell temperature of −5 °C. Fig. 9 demonstrates this change in silicone fluid inlet temperature on cell preheating performance parameters. As the fluid inlet temperature increases, the warming rate escalates. Initially, at a temperature of 30 °C, the rate is 0.54 °C/min. However, when the temperature reaches 50 °C, the rate triples to 1.63 °C/min. Therefore, the inlet temperature has a great influence on the warming rate. The increase in inlet temperature also significantly affects the maximum temperature difference between a single cell and a battery pack. When the fluid temperature is 30 °C, the temperature difference between the two is controlled below 4 °C. However, as the temperature reaches 45 °C and 50 °C, the temperature difference between single cells increases to 7.4 °C and 9.2 °C, respectively. Concurrently, the temperature disparity within the battery pack also ascends to 8.9 °C and 10.9 °C. The entrance temperature is conducive to increasing the rate of temperature rise, it also increases the temperature difference between the cell and the battery pack, which is not conducive to the safe use of LIB. And as the fluid temperature increases, energy consumption is higher than other factors. In summary, it is recommended that the inlet temperature be controlled at 30°C–40 °C.

The experiments set the silicone oil temperature at 40 °C, and the preheating target temperature at 15 °C. The constant temperature chamber is set to −5 °C. The inlet flow quantity is 2.0 L/min, and the cell spacing is at 2 mm, 4 mm, 6 mm, and 8 mm. Fig. 10 presents the effect of different spacing in the cells on the key performance parameters of preheating. When the cell spacing is small, the gap flow is not uniform. This scenario is detrimental to the efficient heat transfer between the fluid and the cell. At this time, increasing the spacing will strengthen the heat transfer. When the spacing increased from 2 mm to 4 mm, the rate of temperature increased from 0.97 °C/min to 1.23 °C/min. When spacing exceeds 4 mm, the gap flow becomes more stable. Consequently, the rate of temperature increase compared to the 4 mm has decreased and tends to stabilize to 1.15 °C/min or so. As the heat transfer is enhanced by increasing cell spacing, the maximum temperature difference of the cells increases, and the flow stabilizes after the temperature difference is slightly reduced. The temperature difference of the single battery from 3.6 °C to 5.5 °C stabilizes at 5.4 °C, while the temperature difference of the battery group from 5.4 °C to 7.9 °C stabilizes at about 8 °C. The maximum temperature difference of the battery pack is more affected by the spacing than that of the single cell. However, the influence of the spacing of the battery group on the maximum temperature difference is gradually decreasing.

In summary, compared with the different preheating methods in Table 1, the advantages of immersion preheating are reflected in the consistency of battery temperature and thermal safety. Contamination can occur due to leakage of immersion preheating fluid. The leakage will contaminate the liquid and affect the battery's preheating. Also, the batteries need some space to keep them spaced apart. Therefore, immersion preheating places strict requirements on space and equipment sealing. For electric vehicles operating in complex environments where equipment sealing is challenging, immersion preheating is unsuitable. Immersion thermal management is more suitable for energy storage where the operating environment is more stable, such as photovoltaic power generation. Immersion thermal management ensures battery thermal safety and thermal management. Finally, future research on immersion preheating will focus on the study of various immersion media, the impact of immersion solutions on battery lifespan, and strategies for reducing energy consumption.

In this study, the enhancement of low-temperature performance in batteries through immersion preheating is examined. Additionally, the impact of various factors on the preheating process and the energy consumption of the preheating system are investigated experimentally. The experimental conclusions are as follows.
(1)
Immersion preheating significantly enhances battery performance at low temperatures. Batteries preheated to higher temperatures exhibit lower discharging performance compared to those at corresponding temperatures. Conversely, at lower target temperatures, the performance of batteries surpasses that of batteries at the respective temperatures.
(2)
The heat utilization efficiency of immersion preheating is low, and the heat loss in the heating process is large. The lower the ambient temperature, the higher the energy consumption. At an ambient temperature of −25 °C, preheating energy consumption has exceeded 80 % of the rated capacity of the battery pack.
(3)
The inlet temperature has a more significant impact on the preheating index compared to other factors. Increasing the inlet temperature enhances the rate of temperature rise but also results in a greater temperature difference. The effect of increasing flow rate and spacing on the temperature rise rate is gradually weakened. Moreover, an increment in the flow rate exacerbates the temperature inhomogeneity within the battery pack, albeit with a minimal effect on the temperature discrepancy among individual cells. The increase in cell spacing has a greater effect on the temperature difference of the battery pack, and the maximum temperature difference can still be controlled at 8 °C.

Jiakang Bao: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhifu Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Wei-Tao Wu: Methodology, Data curation. Lei Wei: Methodology, Formal analysis. Jizu Lyu: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Yang Li: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Heng Huang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Yubai Li: Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization. Yongchen Song: Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52106226, 52176058), supported by Program for Scientific Research Start-up Funds of Guangdong Ocean University (Grant No. 060302062309).
